nodes.name: Least Upper Bound Property Implies Existence of Suprema
nodes.question: State the theorem: Least Upper Bound Property Implies Existence of Suprema.
nodes.statement_display: Let $S$ be an ordered set with the least upper bound property. If $A\subseteq S$ is nonempty and bounded above in $S$, then $A$ has a supremum in $S$.
nodes.statement_tex: Let $S$ be an ordered set with the least upper bound property. If $A\subseteq S$ is nonempty and bounded above in $S$, then $A$ has a supremum in $S$.
nodes.formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let }S\text{ be an ordered set with the least upper bound property.}\\
&\forall A\subseteq S\;
\bigl[
A\ne\varnothing
\land
\exists u\in S\;\forall x\in A\;(x\le u)
\Longrightarrow
\exists s\in S\;(s=\sup_S A)
\bigr].
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let }S\text{ be an ordered set with the least upper bound property.}\\
&\forall A\subseteq S\;
\bigl[
A\ne\varnothing
\land
\exists u\in S\;\forall x\in A\;(x\le u)
\Longrightarrow
\exists s\in S\;(s=\sup_S A)
\bigr].
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\operatorname{LeastUpperBoundProperty}(S)
\quad\text{and}\quad
\exists A\subseteq S:
A\ne\varnothing,\ A\text{ bounded above in }S,\ \text{and }\sup_S A\text{ does not exist}
\]
is impossible.
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
\operatorname{LeastUpperBoundProperty}(S)
\quad\text{and}\quad
\exists A\subseteq S:
A\ne\varnothing,\ A\text{ bounded above in }S,\ \text{and }\sup_S A\text{ does not exist}
\]
is impossible.
nodes.negation_predicate:
\[
\operatorname{LeastUpperBoundProperty}(S)
\land
\exists A\subseteq S\;
\bigl[A\ne\varnothing
\land \operatorname{BoundedAbove}_S(A)
\land
\neg\exists s\in S\;\operatorname{Supremum}_S(s,A)\bigr]
\]
is a contradictory configuration.
nodes.failure_modes: The theorem could fail only if an admissible subset were nonempty and bounded above while still lacking a least upper bound in $S$. That is precisely the situation ruled out by the least upper bound property.
nodes.proof_sketch:
Step 1. Fix a subset to which the property applies.

Assume that $S$ has the Least Upper Bound Property. Let $A \subseteq S$ be nonempty and bounded above in $S$.
Step 2. Match the hypotheses on $A$ with the definition of the Least Upper Bound Property.

The Least Upper Bound Property says that every nonempty subset of $S$ that is bounded above in $S$ has a least upper bound in $S$. The subset $A$ satisfies these hypotheses.
Step 3. Apply the property.

By the Least Upper Bound Property, there exists $s \in S$ such that $s$ is a least upper bound of $A$.
Step 4. Translate to supremum.

By definition, this means $\operatorname{Supremum}(s,A)$.
Step 5. Conclude.

\[
\exists s \in S \; \operatorname{Supremum}(s,A).
\]
proof_steps:
Step 1. Fix a subset to which the property applies.

Assume that $S$ has the Least Upper Bound Property. Let $A \subseteq S$ be nonempty and bounded above in $S$.

Step 2. Match the hypotheses on $A$ with the definition of the Least Upper Bound Property.

The Least Upper Bound Property says that every nonempty subset of $S$ that is bounded above in $S$ has a least upper bound in $S$. The subset $A$ satisfies these hypotheses.

Step 3. Apply the property.

By the Least Upper Bound Property, there exists $s \in S$ such that $s$ is a least upper bound of $A$.

Step 4. Translate to supremum.

By definition, this means $\operatorname{Supremum}(s,A)$.

Step 5. Conclude.

\[
\exists s \in S \; \operatorname{Supremum}(s,A).
\]
nodes.proof_idea: Direct application of the Least Upper Bound Property followed by unpacking the definition of supremum.
nodes.source_text: This theorem is the operational use of the least upper bound property. Once the ambient ordered set has that property, every nonempty bounded-above subset receives the least ceiling that the definition promises.

nodes.name: Upper Bound Property of Supremum
nodes.question: State the proposition: Upper Bound Property of Supremum.
nodes.statement_display: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $s=\sup A$. Then $x\le s$ for every $x\in A$.
nodes.statement_tex: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $s=\sup A$. Then $x\le s$ for every $x\in A$.
nodes.negation_display:
\[
s=\sup A
\quad\text{and}\quad
\exists x\in A\;(s<x)
\]
is impossible.
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
s=\sup A
\quad\text{and}\quad
\exists x\in A\;(s<x)
\]
is impossible.
nodes.failure_modes: The proposition could fail only if a point of $A$ sat strictly above its claimed supremum. Such a point would show that the claimed supremum is not even an upper bound.
nodes.failure_mode_decomposition:
\[
\neg\operatorname{UpperBound}(s,A)
\Longleftrightarrow
\underbrace{\exists x\in A\;(s<x)}_{\text{element above the claimed supremum}}.
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
Step 1. Fix the set and the candidate supremum.

Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $s \in \mathbb{R}$. Assume that
\[
s=\sup A.
\]

The goal is to prove that every element of $A$ lies below or at $s$.
Step 2. Recall the first part of the definition of supremum.

By definition, a supremum of a set is in particular an upper bound of that set. Thus from
\[
s=\sup A
\]
it follows that $s$ is an upper bound of $A$.
Step 3. Unpack what “upper bound” means.

To say that $s$ is an upper bound of $A$ means exactly that
\[
\forall x \in A \; (x \le s).
\]

This is already the desired conclusion.
Step 4. State the result in ordinary mathematical language.

Since
\[
\forall x \in A \; (x \le s),
\]
it follows that
\[
x \le s
\qquad \text{for every } x \in A.
\]

Therefore every element of $A$ lies below the supremum.
proof_steps:
Step 1. Fix the set and the candidate supremum.

Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $s \in \mathbb{R}$. Assume that
\[
s=\sup A.
\]

The goal is to prove that every element of $A$ lies below or at $s$.

Step 2. Recall the first part of the definition of supremum.

By definition, a supremum of a set is in particular an upper bound of that set. Thus from
\[
s=\sup A
\]
it follows that $s$ is an upper bound of $A$.

Step 3. Unpack what “upper bound” means.

To say that $s$ is an upper bound of $A$ means exactly that
\[
\forall x \in A \; (x \le s).
\]

This is already the desired conclusion.

Step 4. State the result in ordinary mathematical language.

Since
\[
\forall x \in A \; (x \le s),
\]
it follows that
\[
x \le s
\qquad \text{for every } x \in A.
\]

Therefore every element of $A$ lies below the supremum.
nodes.proof_idea: This is a direct unpacking proof. The proposition isolates the upper-bound part of the definition of supremum, so the argument consists of invoking the definition and then rewriting the conclusion in ordinary quantified form.
nodes.source_text: The supremum has two jobs: it must bound the set from above, and it must be least among all such bounds. This proposition isolates the first job.

nodes.name: Archimedean Property
nodes.question: State the theorem: Archimedean Property.
nodes.statement_display: For every real number $x$, there exists a natural number $n$ such that $n>x$.
nodes.statement_tex: For every real number $x$, there exists a natural number $n$ such that $n>x$.
nodes.formal:
\[
\forall x\in\mathbb{R}\;\exists n\in\mathbb{N}\;(n>x).
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\forall x\in\mathbb{R}\;\exists n\in\mathbb{N}\;(n>x).
\]
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\exists x\in\mathbb{R}\;\forall n\in\mathbb{N}\;(n\le x).
\]
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
\exists x\in\mathbb{R}\;\forall n\in\mathbb{N}\;(n\le x).
\]
nodes.failure_modes: The theorem fails exactly when the natural numbers are bounded above by some real number.
nodes.failure_mode_decomposition:
\[
\neg\operatorname{ArchimedeanProperty}(\mathbb{R})
=
\underbrace{\exists x\in\mathbb{R}}_{\text{real upper bound}}
\;\underbrace{\forall n\in\mathbb{N}\;(n\le x)}_{\text{all natural numbers remain below it}}.
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
Step 1. Assume the negation for contradiction. We assume that the set of natural numbers $\mathbb{N}$ is bounded above. Formally:
\[ \exists x \in \mathbb{R} \; \forall n \in \mathbb{N} : n \le x \]
Step 2. Invoke the Least Upper Bound Property. Since $\mathbb{N}$ is non-empty ($1 \in \mathbb{N}$) and assumed to be bounded above, the completeness of $\mathbb{R}$ ensures that $s = \sup \mathbb{N}$ exists in $\mathbb{R}$.
Step 3. Apply the $\varepsilon$-characterization of the supremum. Let $\varepsilon = 1$. By the characterization of the supremum, there must be some element of the set strictly greater than $s - \varepsilon$. Thus, there exists $m \in \mathbb{N}$ such that:
\[ m > s - 1 \]
Step 4. Construct a contradiction within the set. Adding $1$ to both sides of the inequality from Step 3:
\[ m + 1 > s \]
By the inductive property of natural numbers, if $m \in \mathbb{N}$, then $m + 1 \in \mathbb{N}$. However, we have found a natural number $m+1$ that is strictly greater than $s$, the supposed upper bound of $\mathbb{N}$.
Step 5. Conclude the proof. The existence of $m+1 > s$ contradicts the definition of $s$ as an upper bound. Therefore, our initial assumption that $\mathbb{N}$ is bounded must be false. Thus, for every $x \in \mathbb{R}$, there exists some $n \in \mathbb{N}$ such that $n > x$.
proof_steps:
Step 1. Assume the negation for contradiction. We assume that the set of natural numbers $\mathbb{N}$ is bounded above. Formally:
\[ \exists x \in \mathbb{R} \; \forall n \in \mathbb{N} : n \le x \]

Step 2. Invoke the Least Upper Bound Property. Since $\mathbb{N}$ is non-empty ($1 \in \mathbb{N}$) and assumed to be bounded above, the completeness of $\mathbb{R}$ ensures that $s = \sup \mathbb{N}$ exists in $\mathbb{R}$.

Step 3. Apply the $\varepsilon$-characterization of the supremum. Let $\varepsilon = 1$. By the characterization of the supremum, there must be some element of the set strictly greater than $s - \varepsilon$. Thus, there exists $m \in \mathbb{N}$ such that:
\[ m > s - 1 \]

Step 4. Construct a contradiction within the set. Adding $1$ to both sides of the inequality from Step 3:
\[ m + 1 > s \]
By the inductive property of natural numbers, if $m \in \mathbb{N}$, then $m + 1 \in \mathbb{N}$. However, we have found a natural number $m+1$ that is strictly greater than $s$, the supposed upper bound of $\mathbb{N}$.

Step 5. Conclude the proof. The existence of $m+1 > s$ contradicts the definition of $s$ as an upper bound. Therefore, our initial assumption that $\mathbb{N}$ is bounded must be false. Thus, for every $x \in \mathbb{R}$, there exists some $n \in \mathbb{N}$ such that $n > x$.
nodes.proof_idea: The proof utilizes a contradiction strategy based on the completeness of the real numbers. It shows that if $\mathbb{N}$ were bounded, its "ceiling" would have to be surpassable by simply adding $1$ to a value near that ceiling.
nodes.source_text: The Archimedean Property says that the natural numbers do not stop below any real ceiling. No real number is so large that every natural number remains below it. In this chapter, the result is a consequence of completeness: a bounded copy of $\mathbb{N}$ would have a supremum, and that supremum is contradicted by shifting one natural number upward.

nodes.text_preview: [Archimedean Property] For every real number $x$, there exists a natural number $n$ such that $n>x$.

nodes.name: Archimedean Reciprocal Corollary
nodes.question: State the corollary: Archimedean Reciprocal Corollary.
nodes.statement_display: For every $x>0$ and every $y\in\mathbb{R}$, there exists $n\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $nx>y$.
nodes.statement_tex: For every $x>0$ and every $y\in\mathbb{R}$, there exists $n\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $nx>y$.
nodes.formal:
\[
\forall x,y\in\mathbb{R}\;\bigl(x>0\rightarrow \exists n\in\mathbb{N}\;(nx>y)\bigr).
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\forall x,y\in\mathbb{R}\;\bigl(x>0\rightarrow \exists n\in\mathbb{N}\;(nx>y)\bigr).
\]
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\exists x,y\in\mathbb{R}\;\bigl(x>0\land \forall n\in\mathbb{N}\;(nx\le y)\bigr).
\]
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
\exists x,y\in\mathbb{R}\;\bigl(x>0\land \forall n\in\mathbb{N}\;(nx\le y)\bigr).
\]
nodes.failure_modes: The corollary fails when a positive real step size has all of its natural multiples bounded above by some real threshold.
nodes.failure_mode_decomposition:
\[
\neg\operatorname{ArchimedeanReciprocal}(\mathbb{R})
=
\underbrace{\exists x\in\mathbb{R}\;(x>0)}_{\text{positive step size}}
\;\land\;
\underbrace{\exists y\in\mathbb{R}\;\forall n\in\mathbb{N}\;(nx\le y)}_{\text{all natural multiples stay bounded}}.
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
Step 1. Identify the target value for the Archimedean Property. We are given a fixed $\varepsilon > 0$. We want to find a natural number $n$ that makes $\frac{1}{n}$ very small.
Step 2. Transform the inequality. The goal is $\frac{1}{n} < \varepsilon$. Since $n > 0$ and $\varepsilon > 0$, this is algebraically equivalent to:
\[ n > \frac{1}{\varepsilon} \]
Let $x = \frac{1}{\varepsilon}$. Since $\varepsilon$ is a real number, $x$ is also a real number.
Step 3. Apply the Archimedean Property. The Archimedean Property states that for any real number $x$, there exists a natural number $n$ such that $n > x$. Applying this to our $x = \frac{1}{\varepsilon}$:
\[ \exists n \in \mathbb{N} : n > \frac{1}{\varepsilon} \]
Step 4. Revert to the reciprocal form. Starting with $n > \frac{1}{\varepsilon}$, we multiply both sides by $\frac{\varepsilon}{n}$ (which is positive, so the inequality direction is preserved):
\[ \varepsilon > \frac{1}{n} \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{1}{n} < \varepsilon \]
Thus, for any positive tolerance $\varepsilon$, we have found a unit fraction $1/n$ that falls within that tolerance.
proof_steps: Step 1. Identify the target value for the Archimedean Property. We are given a fixed $\varepsilon > 0$. We want to find a natural number $n$ that makes $\frac{1}{n}$ very small.

Step 2. Transform the inequality. The goal is $\frac{1}{n} < \varepsilon$. Since $n > 0$ and $\varepsilon > 0$, this is algebraically equivalent to:
\[ n > \frac{1}{\varepsilon} \]
Let $x = \frac{1}{\varepsilon}$. Since $\varepsilon$ is a real number, $x$ is also a real number.

Step 3. Apply the Archimedean Property. The Archimedean Property states that for any real number $x$, there exists a natural number $n$ such that $n > x$. Applying this to our $x = \frac{1}{\varepsilon}$:
\[ \exists n \in \mathbb{N} : n > \frac{1}{\varepsilon} \]

Step 4. Revert to the reciprocal form. Starting with $n > \frac{1}{\varepsilon}$, we multiply both sides by $\frac{\varepsilon}{n}$ (which is positive, so the inequality direction is preserved):
\[ \varepsilon > \frac{1}{n} \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{1}{n} < \varepsilon \]
Thus, for any positive tolerance $\varepsilon$, we have found a unit fraction $1/n$ that falls within that tolerance.
nodes.proof_idea: This is a direct proof utilizing the Archimedean Property of $\mathbb{R}$.
nodes.source_text: Any fixed positive real step eventually passes any real threshold when repeated enough times. The statement is the Archimedean Property applied after rescaling by the positive number $x$: growth by units becomes growth by steps of size $x$.

nodes.name: Least Upper Bound Property
nodes.question: State the definition: Least Upper Bound Property.
nodes.statement_display: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set. The set $S$ has the \emph{Least Upper Bound Property} if and only if every nonempty subset of $S$ that is bounded above in $S$ has a supremum in $S$.
nodes.statement_tex: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set. The set $S$ has the \emph{Least Upper Bound Property} if and only if every nonempty subset of $S$ that is bounded above in $S$ has a supremum in $S$.
nodes.proposition:
\[
\operatorname{LeastUpperBoundProperty}(S)\coloneqq
\forall A\subseteq S\;
\Bigl[
\bigl(A\neq\varnothing \land \exists u\in S\;\forall x\in A\;(x\le u)\bigr)
\rightarrow
\exists s\in S\;\operatorname{Supremum}(s,A)
\Bigr].
\]
nodes.formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let }(S,\le)\text{ be an ordered set}.\\
&S\text{ has the Least Upper Bound Property}\\
&\Longleftrightarrow
\forall A\subseteq S\;
\Bigl[
\bigl(A\neq\varnothing \land \exists u\in S\;\forall x\in A\;(x\le u)\bigr)
\rightarrow
\exists s\in S\;\operatorname{Supremum}(s,A)
\Bigr].
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let }(S,\le)\text{ be an ordered set}.\\
&S\text{ has the Least Upper Bound Property}\\
&\Longleftrightarrow
\forall A\subseteq S\;
\Bigl[
\bigl(A\neq\varnothing \land \exists u\in S\;\forall x\in A\;(x\le u)\bigr)
\rightarrow
\exists s\in S\;\operatorname{Supremum}(s,A)
\Bigr].
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let }(S,\le)\text{ be an ordered set}.\\
&S\text{ does not have the Least Upper Bound Property}\\
&\Longleftrightarrow
\exists A\subseteq S\;
\Bigl[
A\neq\varnothing
\land
\exists u\in S\;\forall x\in A\;(x\le u)
\land
\forall s\in S\;\neg\operatorname{Supremum}(s,A)
\Bigr].
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let }(S,\le)\text{ be an ordered set}.\\
&S\text{ does not have the Least Upper Bound Property}\\
&\Longleftrightarrow
\exists A\subseteq S\;
\Bigl[
A\neq\varnothing
\land
\exists u\in S\;\forall x\in A\;(x\le u)
\land
\forall s\in S\;\neg\operatorname{Supremum}(s,A)
\Bigr].
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_predicate:
\[
\neg\operatorname{LeastUpperBoundProperty}(S)\Longleftrightarrow
\exists A\subseteq S\;
\Bigl[
A\neq\varnothing
\land
\exists u\in S\;\forall x\in A\;(x\le u)
\land
\forall s\in S\;\neg\operatorname{Supremum}(s,A)
\Bigr].
\]
nodes.failure_modes: The property fails when there is a nonempty subset of the ambient ordered set that has at least one upper bound but has no least upper bound inside that same ambient set.
nodes.source_text: The Least Upper Bound Property says that upper-bounded nonempty subsets do not point toward missing ceiling elements. Whenever the order supplies at least one upper bound, it also supplies a smallest upper bound in the same ambient set. Failure therefore indicates a hole in the ordered structure rather than a defect in the subset alone.

nodes.name: Completeness of $ $
nodes.question: State the axiom: Completeness of $ $.
nodes.statement_display: The ordered field $\mathbb{R}$ has the Least Upper Bound Property.
nodes.statement_tex: The ordered field $\mathbb{R}$ has the Least Upper Bound Property.
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\neg\operatorname{LeastUpperBoundProperty}(\mathbb{R})\\
&\Longleftrightarrow
\exists A\subseteq\mathbb{R}\;
\Bigl[
A\neq\varnothing
\land
\exists u\in\mathbb{R}\;\forall x\in A\;(x\le u)
\land
\forall s\in\mathbb{R}\;\neg\operatorname{Supremum}(s,A)
\Bigr].
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\neg\operatorname{LeastUpperBoundProperty}(\mathbb{R})\\
&\Longleftrightarrow
\exists A\subseteq\mathbb{R}\;
\Bigl[
A\neq\varnothing
\land
\exists u\in\mathbb{R}\;\forall x\in A\;(x\le u)
\land
\forall s\in\mathbb{R}\;\neg\operatorname{Supremum}(s,A)
\Bigr].
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_predicate:
\[
\neg\operatorname{LeastUpperBoundProperty}(\mathbb{R})\Longleftrightarrow
\exists A\subseteq\mathbb{R}\;
\Bigl[
A\neq\varnothing
\land
\exists u\in\mathbb{R}\;\forall x\in A\;(x\le u)
\land
\forall s\in\mathbb{R}\;\neg\operatorname{Supremum}(s,A)
\Bigr].
\]
nodes.failure_modes: Completeness would fail if some nonempty subset of $\mathbb{R}$ were bounded above in $\mathbb{R}$ but had no supremum in $\mathbb{R}$.
nodes.source_text: The completeness axiom asserts that the real line has no order gaps of the least-upper-bound kind. Nonempty sets of real numbers that are bounded above always determine a real number sitting exactly at the least possible ceiling. This is the structural property that distinguishes $\mathbb{R}$ from ordered fields such as $\mathbb{Q}$.
nodes.text_preview: [Completeness of $ $] The ordered field $ $ has the Least Upper Bound Property.

nodes.name: Dense Subset
nodes.question: State the definition: Dense Subset.
nodes.statement_display: Let $D\subseteq S\subseteq\mathbb{R}$. The set $D$ is \emph{dense in} $S$ if and only if for every $x\in S$ and every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists $d\in D$ such that $|x-d|<\varepsilon$.
nodes.statement_tex: Let $D\subseteq S\subseteq\mathbb{R}$. The set $D$ is \emph{dense in} $S$ if and only if for every $x\in S$ and every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists $d\in D$ such that $|x-d|<\varepsilon$.
nodes.formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let }D\subseteq S\subseteq\mathbb{R}.\\
&D\text{ is dense in }S
\Longleftrightarrow
\forall x\in S\;\forall \varepsilon>0\;\exists d\in D\;\bigl(|x-d|<\varepsilon\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let }D\subseteq S\subseteq\mathbb{R}.\\
&D\text{ is dense in }S
\Longleftrightarrow
\forall x\in S\;\forall \varepsilon>0\;\exists d\in D\;\bigl(|x-d|<\varepsilon\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let }D\subseteq S\subseteq\mathbb{R}.\\
&D\text{ is not dense in }S
\Longleftrightarrow
\exists x\in S\;\exists \varepsilon_0>0\;\forall d\in D\;\bigl(|x-d|\ge \varepsilon_0\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let }D\subseteq S\subseteq\mathbb{R}.\\
&D\text{ is not dense in }S
\Longleftrightarrow
\exists x\in S\;\exists \varepsilon_0>0\;\forall d\in D\;\bigl(|x-d|\ge \varepsilon_0\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.failure_modes: Density fails when some point of $S$ has a positive-radius neighborhood that contains no point of $D$.
nodes.failure_mode_decomposition:
\[
\neg\operatorname{DenseSubset}(D,S)
=
\underbrace{\exists x\in S}_{\text{point not approximated}}
\;\underbrace{\exists \varepsilon_0>0}_{\text{positive exclusion radius}}
\;\underbrace{\forall d\in D\;\bigl(|x-d|\ge \varepsilon_0\bigr)}_{\text{no point of }D\text{ enters the neighborhood}}.
\]
nodes.source_text: Density is an approximation property inside $S$. It does not say that every point of $S$ already belongs to $D$; it says that points of $D$ occur arbitrarily close to every point of $S$. Thus $D$ can be a proper subset of $S$ while still leaving no positive-width gap inside $S$.
nodes.text_preview: [Dense Subset] Let $D S $. The set $D$ is $S$ if and only if for every $x S$ and every $ >0$ there exists $d D$ such that $|x-d|< $.

nodes.name: Order Dense Subset
nodes.question: State the definition: Order Dense Subset.
nodes.statement_display: Let $X$ be a linearly ordered set and let $A\subseteq X$. The subset $A$ is \emph{order dense in} $X$ if and only if for every $a,b\in X$ with $a<b$ there exists $c\in A$ such that $a<c<b$.
nodes.statement_tex: Let $X$ be a linearly ordered set and let $A\subseteq X$. The subset $A$ is \emph{order dense in} $X$ if and only if for every $a,b\in X$ with $a<b$ there exists $c\in A$ such that $a<c<b$.
nodes.formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let }X\text{ be a linearly ordered set and }A\subseteq X.\\
&A\text{ is order dense in }X
\Longleftrightarrow
\forall a,b\in X\;\bigl(a<b\rightarrow \exists c\in A\;(a<c<b)\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let }X\text{ be a linearly ordered set and }A\subseteq X.\\
&A\text{ is order dense in }X
\Longleftrightarrow
\forall a,b\in X\;\bigl(a<b\rightarrow \exists c\in A\;(a<c<b)\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let }X\text{ be a linearly ordered set and }A\subseteq X.\\
&A\text{ is not order dense in }X
\Longleftrightarrow
\exists a,b\in X\;\bigl(a<b\land \forall c\in A\;(c\le a\lor b\le c)\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let }X\text{ be a linearly ordered set and }A\subseteq X.\\
&A\text{ is not order dense in }X
\Longleftrightarrow
\exists a,b\in X\;\bigl(a<b\land \forall c\in A\;(c\le a\lor b\le c)\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_predicate:
\[
\neg\operatorname{OrderDenseSubset}(A,X)\Longleftrightarrow
\exists a,b\in X\;\bigl(a<b\land \forall c\in A\;(c\le a\lor b\le c)\bigr).
\]
nodes.failure_modes: Order density fails when there is a nonempty open interval of $X$ that contains no point of $A$.
nodes.failure_mode_decomposition:
\[
\neg\operatorname{OrderDenseSubset}(A,X)
=
\underbrace{\exists a,b\in X\;(a<b)}_{\text{nonempty interval}}
\;\land\;
\underbrace{\forall c\in A\;(c\le a\lor b\le c)}_{\text{no point of }A\text{ lies between }a\text{ and }b}.
\]
nodes.source_text: Order density is an interval-penetration property. It ignores metric distance and asks only whether every ordered gap contains a point of the subset. A subset can therefore be order dense in $X$ precisely when no nonempty open interval of $X$ is missed entirely by that subset.

nodes.statement_display: For every $a,b\in\mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$, there exists $q\in\mathbb{Q}$ such that $a<q<b$.
nodes.statement_tex: For every $a,b\in\mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$, there exists $q\in\mathbb{Q}$ such that $a<q<b$.
nodes.formal:
\[
\forall a,b\in\mathbb{R}\;\bigl(a<b\rightarrow \exists q\in\mathbb{Q}\;(a<q<b)\bigr).
\]

\]
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\exists a,b\in\mathbb{R}\;\bigl(a<b\land \forall q\in\mathbb{Q}\;(q\le a\lor b\le q)\bigr).
\]
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
\exists a,b\in\mathbb{R}\;\bigl(a<b\land \forall q\in\mathbb{Q}\;(q\le a\lor b\le q)\bigr).
\]

\[
\neg\operatorname{OrderDenseSubset}(\mathbb{Q},\mathbb{R})
=
\underbrace{\exists a,b\in\mathbb{R}\;(a<b)}_{\text{nonempty real interval}}
\;\land\;
\underbrace{\forall q\in\mathbb{Q}\;(q\le a\lor b\le q)}_{\text{no rational lies inside}}.
\]

nodes.source_text: Rational numbers penetrate every real interval. The theorem does not say that every real number is rational; it says that rational numbers are never separated from a real interval by a positive order gap. This is the interval form of rational density in the real line.

nodes.name: Nested Interval Property
nodes.question: State the theorem: Nested Interval Property.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(I_n)$ be a sequence of nonempty closed intervals in $\mathbb{R}$. If $I_{n+1}\subseteq I_n$ for every $n\in\mathbb{N}$, then
\[
 \bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} I_n\neq\varnothing.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(I_n)$ be a sequence of nonempty closed intervals in $\mathbb{R}$. If $I_{n+1}\subseteq I_n$ for every $n\in\mathbb{N}$, then
\[
 \bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} I_n\neq\varnothing.
\]
nodes.formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\forall (a_n)\subseteq\mathbb{R}\;\forall (b_n)\subseteq\mathbb{R}\;\\
&\Bigl[
\bigl(\forall n\in\mathbb{N}\;(a_n\le b_n)\bigr)
\land
\bigl(\forall n\in\mathbb{N}\;([a_{n+1},b_{n+1}]\subseteq [a_n,b_n])\bigr)
\\
&\qquad\rightarrow
\exists x\in\mathbb{R}\;\forall n\in\mathbb{N}\;(x\in [a_n,b_n])
\Bigr].
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\forall (a_n)\subseteq\mathbb{R}\;\forall (b_n)\subseteq\mathbb{R}\;\\
&\Bigl[
\bigl(\forall n\in\mathbb{N}\;(a_n\le b_n)\bigr)
\land
\bigl(\forall n\in\mathbb{N}\;([a_{n+1},b_{n+1}]\subseteq [a_n,b_n])\bigr)
\\
&\qquad\rightarrow
\exists x\in\mathbb{R}\;\forall n\in\mathbb{N}\;(x\in [a_n,b_n])
\Bigr].
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\exists (a_n)\subseteq\mathbb{R}\;\exists (b_n)\subseteq\mathbb{R}\;\\
&\Bigl[
\bigl(\forall n\in\mathbb{N}\;(a_n\le b_n)\bigr)
\land
\bigl(\forall n\in\mathbb{N}\;([a_{n+1},b_{n+1}]\subseteq [a_n,b_n])\bigr)
\\
&\qquad\land
\forall x\in\mathbb{R}\;\exists n\in\mathbb{N}\;(x\notin [a_n,b_n])
\Bigr].
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\exists (a_n)\subseteq\mathbb{R}\;\exists (b_n)\subseteq\mathbb{R}\;\\
&\Bigl[
\bigl(\forall n\in\mathbb{N}\;(a_n\le b_n)\bigr)
\land
\bigl(\forall n\in\mathbb{N}\;([a_{n+1},b_{n+1}]\subseteq [a_n,b_n])\bigr)
\\
&\qquad\land
\forall x\in\mathbb{R}\;\exists n\in\mathbb{N}\;(x\notin [a_n,b_n])
\Bigr].
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.failure_modes: The theorem fails when a nested sequence of nonempty closed real intervals has no common real point.
nodes.proof_sketch:
Step 1. Analyze the endpoints of the nested intervals. By the definition of nesting, $[a_{n+1}, b_{n+1}] \subseteq [a_n, b_n]$. This implies the following ordering:
\[ a_n \le a_{n+1} \le b_{n+1} \le b_n \]
This tells us that $(a_n)$ is a $\operatorname{MonotoneIncreasingSequence}$ and $(b_n)$ is a $\operatorname{MonotoneDecreasingSequence}$.
Step 2. Establish a bound for the sequence of left endpoints. Every $b_k$ serves as an upper bound for the entire set $\{a_n : n \in \mathbb{N}\}$. To see this, if $n \le k$, then $a_n \le a_k \le b_k$. If $n > k$, then $a_n \le b_n \le b_k$. Thus, $a_n \le b_k$ for all $n, k$.
Step 3. Invoke the Least Upper Bound property. Let $A = \{a_n : n \in \mathbb{N}\}$. Since $A$ is non-empty and bounded above (by $b_1$), $x = \sup A$ exists in $\mathbb{R}$.
Step 4. Show the supremum is contained in every interval. By definition of the supremum as an upper bound, $a_n \le x$ for all $n$.
From Step 2, we know that every $b_n$ is an upper bound for $A$. By the "leastness" property of the supremum, $x \le b_n$ for all $n$.
Combining these, we have:
\[ a_n \le x \le b_n \implies x \in [a_n, b_n] \text{ for all } n \in \mathbb{N} \]
Step 5. Conclude non-empty intersection. Since $x$ is in every $I_n$, it belongs to their intersection. Thus, $\bigcap I_n$ is not empty.
proof_steps:
Step 1. Analyze the endpoints of the nested intervals. By the definition of nesting, $[a_{n+1}, b_{n+1}] \subseteq [a_n, b_n]$. This implies the following ordering:
\[ a_n \le a_{n+1} \le b_{n+1} \le b_n \]
This tells us that $(a_n)$ is a $\operatorname{MonotoneIncreasingSequence}$ and $(b_n)$ is a $\operatorname{MonotoneDecreasingSequence}$.

Step 2. Establish a bound for the sequence of left endpoints. Every $b_k$ serves as an upper bound for the entire set $\{a_n : n \in \mathbb{N}\}$. To see this, if $n \le k$, then $a_n \le a_k \le b_k$. If $n > k$, then $a_n \le b_n \le b_k$. Thus, $a_n \le b_k$ for all $n, k$.
Step 3. Invoke the Least Upper Bound property. Let $A = \{a_n : n \in \mathbb{N}\}$. Since $A$ is non-empty and bounded above (by $b_1$), $x = \sup A$ exists in $\mathbb{R}$.

Step 4. Show the supremum is contained in every interval. By definition of the supremum as an upper bound, $a_n \le x$ for all $n$.
From Step 2, we know that every $b_n$ is an upper bound for $A$. By the "leastness" property of the supremum, $x \le b_n$ for all $n$.
Combining these, we have:
\[ a_n \le x \le b_n \implies x \in [a_n, b_n] \text{ for all } n \in \mathbb{N} \]
Step 5. Conclude non-empty intersection. Since $x$ is in every $I_n$, it belongs to their intersection. Thus, $\bigcap I_n$ is not empty.
nodes.proof_idea: The proof utilizes the Monotone Convergence/Least Upper Bound property to identify the supremum of the left endpoints as a point that is caught between the "rising floor" and the "falling ceiling" of the intervals.
nodes.source_text: Nested closed intervals cannot keep shrinking or relocating in a way that loses every real point. Nesting keeps the intervals aligned, nonemptiness prevents trivial collapse at each stage, and completeness supplies a real point that survives all stages at once.
nodes.text_preview: [Nested Interval Property] Let $(I_n)$ be a sequence of nonempty closed intervals in $ $. If $I_ n+1 I_n$ for every $n $, then \[ _ n=1 ^ I_n . \]

nodes.name: Step Function Approximation
nodes.question: State the theorem: Step Function Approximation.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous and let $\varepsilon>0$.
Then there exists a step function $s_\varepsilon:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ such that
\[
\sup_{x\in[a,b]} |f(x)-s_\varepsilon(x)|<\varepsilon.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous and let $\varepsilon>0$.
Then there exists a step function $s_\varepsilon:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ such that
\[
\sup_{x\in[a,b]} |f(x)-s_\varepsilon(x)|<\varepsilon.
\]
nodes.failure_modes: The statement fails precisely when a continuous $f$ and tolerance $\varepsilon$ are fixed but every step function leaves an error of at least $\varepsilon$ at some point in $[a,b]$.
nodes.failure_mode_decomposition:
\[
\underbrace{\forall s:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R} \text{ step function }}_{\text{all approximants}}
\underbrace{\exists x\in[a,b]\;(|f(x)-s(x)|\ge\varepsilon)}_{\text{witnessed large deviation}}.
\]
nodes.source_text: Uniform continuity on $[a,b]$ guarantees that $f$ does not oscillate rapidly at small scales, so subdividing the interval finely enough allows a step function to track $f$ within any prescribed uniform error. The typical construction samples $f$ at one point of each subinterval and keeps that value constant across the piece. Failure would mean that no matter how the interval is partitioned, some subinterval forces an error at least $\varepsilon$, contradicting uniform continuity. The result is a foundational approximation fact used to show that continuous functions on compact intervals are Riemann integrable and to connect continuous functions with simple combinatorial models.
nodes.text_preview: [Step Function Approximation] Let $f:[a,b] $ be continuous and let $ >0$. Then there exists a step function $s_ :[a,b] $ such that \[ _ x |f(x)-s_ (x)|< . \]

nodes.name: Piecewise Linear Function
nodes.question: State the definition: Piecewise Linear Function.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$ and let $g:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$.
The function $g$ is piecewise linear if and only if there exist
$m \in \mathbb{N}$ with $m \ge 1$ and points
$a=x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_m=b$ such that $g|_{[x_{k-1},x_k]}$ is affine
for every $k\in\{1,\ldots,m\}$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$ and let $g:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$.
The function $g$ is piecewise linear if and only if there exist
$m \in \mathbb{N}$ with $m \ge 1$ and points
$a=x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_m=b$ such that $g|_{[x_{k-1},x_k]}$ is affine
for every $k\in\{1,\ldots,m\}$.
nodes.proposition:
\[
\operatorname{PiecewiseLinear}(g,[a,b]) \coloneqq
\exists m \in \mathbb{N}\;\Bigl(m \ge 1 \land
\exists x_0,\ldots,x_m \in [a,b]\;
\bigl[a=x_0 < \cdots < x_m = b \land
\forall k \in \{1,\ldots,m\}\; g|_{[x_{k-1},x_k]} \text{ is affine}\bigr]\Bigr).
\]
nodes.formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } a,b \in \mathbb{R} \text{ with } a<b \text{ and } g:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}.\\
&g \text{ is piecewise linear}\\
&\Longleftrightarrow
\exists m \in \mathbb{N}\;\Bigl(m \ge 1 \land
\exists x_0,\ldots,x_m \in [a,b]\;
\bigl[a=x_0 < x_1 < \cdots < x_m = b\\
&\qquad\qquad\land
\forall k \in \{1,\ldots,m\}\; g|_{[x_{k-1},x_k]} \text{ is affine}\bigr]\Bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } a,b \in \mathbb{R} \text{ with } a<b \text{ and } g:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}.\\
&g \text{ is piecewise linear}\\
&\Longleftrightarrow
\exists m \in \mathbb{N}\;\Bigl(m \ge 1 \land
\exists x_0,\ldots,x_m \in [a,b]\;
\bigl[a=x_0 < x_1 < \cdots < x_m = b\\
&\qquad\qquad\land
\forall k \in \{1,\ldots,m\}\; g|_{[x_{k-1},x_k]} \text{ is affine}\bigr]\Bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } a,b \in \mathbb{R} \text{ with } a<b \text{ and } g:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}.\\
&g \text{ is not piecewise linear}\\
&\Longleftrightarrow
\forall m \in \mathbb{N}\;\Bigl(m \ge 1 \Rightarrow
\forall x_0,\ldots,x_m \in [a,b]\;
\bigl[a=x_0 < \cdots < x_m = b\\
&\qquad\qquad\Rightarrow
\exists k \in \{1,\ldots,m\}\; g|_{[x_{k-1},x_k]} \text{ is not affine}\bigr]\Bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } a,b \in \mathbb{R} \text{ with } a<b \text{ and } g:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}.\\
&g \text{ is not piecewise linear}\\
&\Longleftrightarrow
\forall m \in \mathbb{N}\;\Bigl(m \ge 1 \Rightarrow
\forall x_0,\ldots,x_m \in [a,b]\;
\bigl[a=x_0 < \cdots < x_m = b\\
&\qquad\qquad\Rightarrow
\exists k \in \{1,\ldots,m\}\; g|_{[x_{k-1},x_k]} \text{ is not affine}\bigr]\Bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]

\[
\neg\operatorname{PiecewiseLinear}(g,[a,b])
\Longleftrightarrow
\forall m \in \mathbb{N}\;\Bigl(m \ge 1 \Rightarrow
\forall x_0,\ldots,x_m \in [a,b]\;
\bigl[a=x_0 < \cdots < x_m = b
\Rightarrow
\exists k \in \{1,\ldots,m\}\; g|_{[x_{k-1},x_k]} \text{ is not affine}\bigr]\Bigr).
\]
nodes.failure_modes:
A proposed piecewise-linear structure can fail because no finite ordered
partition of $[a,b]$ is available, or because for every such partition
at least one subinterval has a restriction of $g$ that is not affine.
nodes.source_text:
A piecewise linear function on $[a,b]$ has a graph made from finitely
many straight line segments. The definition permits different affine
rules on different subintervals, but requires a finite ordered list of
nodes that covers the whole interval.

nodes.name: Piecewise Linear Approximation
nodes.question: State the theorem: Piecewise Linear Approximation.
nodes.statement_display: Let $f\colon[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous and let $\varepsilon>0$. Then there exists a continuous piecewise linear function $\ell_\varepsilon\colon[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ such that $\sup_{x\in[a,b]}|f(x)-\ell_\varepsilon(x)|<\varepsilon$. \hyperref[prf:piecewise-linear-approximation]{Go to proof.}
nodes.statement_tex: Let $f\colon[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous and let $\varepsilon>0$. Then there exists a continuous piecewise linear function $\ell_\varepsilon\colon[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ such that $\sup_{x\in[a,b]}|f(x)-\ell_\varepsilon(x)|<\varepsilon$. \hyperref[prf:piecewise-linear-approximation]{Go to proof.}
nodes.formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } f\colon[a,b]\to\mathbb{R},\; f \text{ continuous},\; \varepsilon>0.\\
&\exists\text{ continuous piecewise linear }\ell_\varepsilon\colon[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}:
\sup_{x\in[a,b]}|f(x)-\ell_\varepsilon(x)|<\varepsilon.
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } f\colon[a,b]\to\mathbb{R},\; f \text{ continuous},\; \varepsilon>0.\\
&\exists\text{ continuous piecewise linear }\ell_\varepsilon\colon[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}:
\sup_{x\in[a,b]}|f(x)-\ell_\varepsilon(x)|<\varepsilon.
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.source_text: Every continuous function on a compact interval can be uniformly approximated by a polygonal curve matching finitely many sampled values of the original graph. The construction refines the interval into a sufficiently fine partition guaranteed by compactness, then connects the sampled values with straight segments, producing a uniformly small error over the entire domain. Failure would require a uniform oscillation exceeding $\varepsilon$ on some subinterval, which cannot occur once the partition is fine enough. Geometrically, the theorem states that continuous graphs admit arbitrarily close polyline shadows, providing a bridge from rough step approximations to smooth polynomial approximations.
nodes.text_preview: [Piecewise Linear Approximation] Let $f $ be continuous and let $ >0$. Then there exists a continuous piecewise linear function $ _ $ such that $ _ x |f(x)- _ (x)|< $.

nodes.name: Weierstrass Approximation Theorem
nodes.question: State the theorem: Weierstrass Approximation Theorem.
nodes.statement_display: Let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous. For every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists a polynomial $p_\varepsilon\in\mathbb{R}[x]$ such that $\|f-p_\varepsilon\|_{\infty,[a,b]}<\varepsilon$.
nodes.statement_tex: Let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous. For every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists a polynomial $p_\varepsilon\in\mathbb{R}[x]$ such that $\|f-p_\varepsilon\|_{\infty,[a,b]}<\varepsilon$.
nodes.source_text: Every continuous function on a closed interval can be uniformly approximated by polynomials, so polynomials are dense in the space of continuous functions under the supremum norm. The result holds because convolutions with Bernstein polynomials preserve continuity and converge uniformly to the original function. Failure would mean there exists a uniform tolerance that no polynomial can match, contradicting the approximation scheme built from Bernstein polynomials. Geometrically, the theorem states that the graph of a continuous function can be matched arbitrarily well by the graph of a polynomial, so polynomial graphs form a flexible mesh that can shadow any continuous curve on a compact interval.
nodes.text_preview: [Weierstrass Approximation Theorem] Let $f:[a,b] $ be continuous. For every $ >0$ there exists a polynomial $p_ [x]$ such that $\|f-p_ \|_ ,[a,b] < $.

nodes.name: Bernstein Polynomial
nodes.question: State the definition: Bernstein Polynomial.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $n \in \mathbb{N}$ and let $f:[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}$.
A function $B_n:[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}$ is called the $n$th Bernstein polynomial of $f$
if and only if, for every $x \in [0,1]$,
\[
B_n(x)=\sum_{k=0}^{n} f\!\left(\frac{k}{n}\right)\binom{n}{k}x^k(1-x)^{n-k}.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $n \in \mathbb{N}$ and let $f:[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}$.
A function $B_n:[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}$ is called the $n$th Bernstein polynomial of $f$
if and only if, for every $x \in [0,1]$,
\[
B_n(x)=\sum_{k=0}^{n} f\!\left(\frac{k}{n}\right)\binom{n}{k}x^k(1-x)^{n-k}.
\]
nodes.source_text: The nth Bernstein polynomial samples f at the uniformly spaced nodes k/n, weights those samples by the binomial probabilities of order n, and produces for each x an average that depends polynomially on x. This construction smooths f into a polynomial that agrees with f at the endpoints and approximates f uniformly as n grows, so describing it explicitly is essential for subsequent approximation theorems. Failure arises only when the candidate polynomial disagrees with the binomial averaging recipe at some point of the unit interval, reflecting an incorrect coefficient or evaluation in the polynomial expression.
nodes.text_preview: [Bernstein Polynomial] Let $n $ and let $f:[0,1] $. A function $B_n:[0,1] $ is called the $n$th Bernstein polynomial of $f$ if and only if, for every $x [0,1]$, \[ B_n(x)= _ k=0 ^ n f\! ( n ) k x^k(1-x)^ n-k . \]

nodes.name: Bernstein Approximation Theorem
nodes.question: State the theorem: Bernstein Approximation Theorem.
nodes.statement_display: Let $f:[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous, and for each $n\in\mathbb{N}$ let $B_n$ denote the $n$th Bernstein polynomial of $f$. Then for every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists $n_\varepsilon\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $\|f-B_n\|_\infty<\varepsilon$ for all $n\ge n_\varepsilon$. \hyperref[prf:bernstein-approximation]{Go to proof.}
nodes.statement_tex: Let $f:[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous, and for each $n\in\mathbb{N}$ let $B_n$ denote the $n$th Bernstein polynomial of $f$. Then for every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists $n_\varepsilon\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $\|f-B_n\|_\infty<\varepsilon$ for all $n\ge n_\varepsilon$. \hyperref[prf:bernstein-approximation]{Go to proof.}
nodes.source_text: The theorem asserts that the Bernstein polynomials of a continuous function on $[0,1]$ converge uniformly to the original function, so the entire graph of $f$ can be approximated to any prescribed sup-norm accuracy by explicit degree-$n$ polynomials once $n$ is large enough. The proof works by showing that the Bernstein operators preserve positivity and linear growth while averaging values of $f$, which forces the uniform error to vanish as $n\to\infty$. This matters because it gives an elementary constructive route from continuous functions to polynomials, strengthening the Weierstrass approximation theorem with a concrete approximating sequence. The approximation can fail only when $f$ is not continuous on $[0,1]$, in which case the Bernstein polynomials may smooth out jumps and no longer capture the original function in the sup norm. Geometrically, each $B_n$ is a convex combination of the samples $f(k/n)$, so the polygons formed by these samples become finer and eventually hug the graph of $f$ everywhere on the interval.
nodes.text_preview: [Bernstein Approximation Theorem] Let $f:[0,1] $ be continuous, and for each $n $ let $B_n$ denote the $n$th Bernstein polynomial of $f$. Then for every $ >0$ there exists $n_ $ such that $\|f-B_n\|_ < $ for all $n n_ $.

nodes.name: Partition
nodes.question: State the definition: Partition.
nodes.statement_display: Let $a<b$ and set $I=[a,b]$. A finite ordered set $P=\{x_0,\ldots,x_n\}$ with $n\in\mathbb{N}$ and $n\ge 1$ is a partition of $I$ if and only if $x_0=a$, $x_n=b$, and $x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_n$. In this case the closed subintervals $[x_{i-1},x_i]$ for $i=1,\ldots,n$ subdivide $I$.
nodes.statement_tex: Let $a<b$ and set $I=[a,b]$. A finite ordered set $P=\{x_0,\ldots,x_n\}$ with $n\in\mathbb{N}$ and $n\ge 1$ is a partition of $I$ if and only if $x_0=a$, $x_n=b$, and $x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_n$. In this case the closed subintervals $[x_{i-1},x_i]$ for $i=1,\ldots,n$ subdivide $I$.
nodes.proposition:
\[
\operatorname{PartitionOfInterval}(P,I) \coloneqq
\exists n\in\mathbb{N}\;\Bigl(n\ge 1 \land P=\{x_0,\ldots,x_n\}\subseteq I
\land x_0=\min I \land x_n=\max I
\land \forall i\in\{1,\ldots,n\}\;(x_{i-1}<x_i)\Bigr).
\]
nodes.formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } a<b,\; I=[a,b],\; n\in\mathbb{N},\; n\ge 1,\; \text{and } P=\{x_0,\ldots,x_n\}\subseteq I.\\
&P \text{ is a partition of } I\\
&\Longleftrightarrow \bigl(x_0=a \land x_n=b \land \forall i\in\{1,\ldots,n\}\;(x_{i-1}<x_i)\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } a<b,\; I=[a,b],\; n\in\mathbb{N},\; n\ge 1,\; \text{and } P=\{x_0,\ldots,x_n\}\subseteq I.\\
&P \text{ is a partition of } I\\
&\Longleftrightarrow \bigl(x_0=a \land x_n=b \land \forall i\in\{1,\ldots,n\}\;(x_{i-1}<x_i)\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } a<b,\; I=[a,b],\; n\in\mathbb{N},\; n\ge 1,\; \text{and } P=\{x_0,\ldots,x_n\}\subseteq I.\\
&P \text{ is not a partition of } I\\
&\Longleftrightarrow \bigl((x_0\ne a) \lor (x_n\ne b) \lor \exists i\in\{1,\ldots,n\}\;(x_{i-1}\ge x_i)\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } a<b,\; I=[a,b],\; n\in\mathbb{N},\; n\ge 1,\; \text{and } P=\{x_0,\ldots,x_n\}\subseteq I.\\
&P \text{ is not a partition of } I\\
&\Longleftrightarrow \bigl((x_0\ne a) \lor (x_n\ne b) \lor \exists i\in\{1,\ldots,n\}\;(x_{i-1}\ge x_i)\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_predicate:
\[
\neg\operatorname{PartitionOfInterval}(P,I) \Longleftrightarrow
\forall n\in\mathbb{N}\;\Bigl(n\ge 1 \Rightarrow
\bigl(P\ne\{x_0,\ldots,x_n\}\lor P\nsubseteq I
\lor x_0\ne\min I \lor x_n\ne\max I
\lor \exists i\in\{1,\ldots,n\}\;(x_{i-1}\ge x_i)\bigr)\Bigr).
\]
nodes.failure_modes: The definition fails precisely in three ways: the left endpoint is omitted, the right endpoint is omitted, or the sequence of nodes ceases to be strictly increasing, leading to repeated or out-of-order points.
nodes.source_text: A partition records the finite list of nodes that slice a closed interval into adjacent subintervals. The strict ordering ensures that every interior point appears exactly once in this linear traversal, so the induced subintervals $[x_{i-1},x_i]$ tile $[a,b]$ without overlap or gaps. Computations of Riemann sums, Darboux sums, and gauge integrals use partitions as the combinatorial scaffolding that supports sampling and measurement. Failure arises when the endpoints are not captured or when the nodes regress or stagnate, which would leave gaps or redundancies in the subdivision.

nodes.name: Tagged Partition
nodes.question: State the definition: Tagged Partition.
nodes.statement_display: Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a < b$. A list $\dot{\mathcal{P}}=\{([x_{i-1},x_i],t_i)\}_{i=1}^n$ is a tagged partition of $[a,b]$ if and only if the subintervals determine a partition $P=\{[x_{i-1},x_i]\}_{i=1}^n$ of $[a,b]$ with $a=x_0 < x_1 < \cdots < x_n=b$, and each tag satisfies $t_i \in [x_{i-1},x_i]$ for $i=1,\ldots,n$. Equivalently, a tagged partition is exactly a partition $P$ of $[a,b]$ together with one tag chosen inside every subinterval of $P$.
nodes.statement_tex: Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a < b$. A list $\dot{\mathcal{P}}=\{([x_{i-1},x_i],t_i)\}_{i=1}^n$ is a tagged partition of $[a,b]$ if and only if the subintervals determine a partition $P=\{[x_{i-1},x_i]\}_{i=1}^n$ of $[a,b]$ with $a=x_0 < x_1 < \cdots < x_n=b$, and each tag satisfies $t_i \in [x_{i-1},x_i]$ for $i=1,\ldots,n$. Equivalently, a tagged partition is exactly a partition $P$ of $[a,b]$ together with one tag chosen inside every subinterval of $P$.

&\dot{\mathcal{P}} \text{ is a tagged partition of } [a,b]\\
&\Longleftrightarrow \bigl(a=x_0<\cdots<x_n=b \land \forall i \in \{1,\ldots,n\}\;(t_i \in [x_{i-1},x_i])\bigr).

&\dot{\mathcal{P}} \text{ is not a tagged partition of } [a,b]\\

\Bigl(x_0\ne a \lor x_n\ne b \lor

&\Longleftrightarrow
\Bigl(x_0\ne a \lor x_n\ne b \lor
\exists j \in \{1,\ldots,n\}\;(x_{j-1} \ge x_j) \lor
\exists i \in \{1,\ldots,n\}\;(t_i \notin [x_{i-1},x_i])\Bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.failure_modes: A list fails to be a tagged partition either because the subintervals do not form a valid partition (the mesh is disordered or has gaps) or because at least one chosen tag lies outside its designated subinterval.
nodes.source_text: A tagged partition augments the usual subdivision of a closed interval by selecting a representative sampling point inside each component subinterval. These tags serve as evaluation points for Riemann sums and gauge integrals. The construction encapsulates both the geometric slicing of the interval and the analytic data needed for sample values. Failure occurs when the slices no longer tile the interval in order or when a sample point is chosen outside its slice, breaking the correspondence between tags and subintervals.

nodes.name: Gauge
nodes.question: State the definition: Gauge.
nodes.statement_display: Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$ and set $I=[a,b]$. A gauge on $I$ is a function $\delta:I\to(0,\infty)$.
nodes.statement_tex: Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$ and set $I=[a,b]$. A gauge on $I$ is a function $\delta:I\to(0,\infty)$.
nodes.failure_modes:
A purported gauge fails either because it is not a function from $I$ to
\(\mathbb{R}\), or because it takes on a nonpositive value somewhere in
$I$.
nodes.source_text: A gauge assigns to each point of the closed interval a positive radius, encoding how tightly a tagged subinterval must cluster around that point in fine partition arguments. The sole obstruction is positivity: if any value is zero or negative, the construction cannot deliver legitimate local scales, so the gauge structure breaks down.
nodes.text_preview: [Gauge] Let $a,b $ with $a<b$ and set $I=[a,b]$. A gauge on $I$ is a function $ :I (0, )$.

nodes.name: $ $-Fine Partition
nodes.question: State the definition: $ $-Fine Partition.
nodes.statement_display: Let $[a,b]$ be a closed interval, let $\delta:[a,b]\to(0,\infty)$ be a gauge, and let $\dot{\mathcal{P}}=\{([x_{i-1},x_i],t_i)\}_{i=1}^n$ be a tagged partition of $[a,b]$. The tagged partition $\dot{\mathcal{P}}$ is $\delta$-fine if and only if for every $i \in \{1,\dots,n\}$ and every $x \in [x_{i-1},x_i]$ the inequality $|x-t_i|<\delta(t_i)$ holds.
nodes.statement_tex: Let $[a,b]$ be a closed interval, let $\delta:[a,b]\to(0,\infty)$ be a gauge, and let $\dot{\mathcal{P}}=\{([x_{i-1},x_i],t_i)\}_{i=1}^n$ be a tagged partition of $[a,b]$. The tagged partition $\dot{\mathcal{P}}$ is $\delta$-fine if and only if for every $i \in \{1,\dots,n\}$ and every $x \in [x_{i-1},x_i]$ the inequality $|x-t_i|<\delta(t_i)$ holds.
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\exists i \in \{1,\dots,n\}\;\exists x \in [x_{i-1},x_i]\;
\bigl(|x-t_i|\ge \delta(t_i)\bigr).
\]
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
\exists i \in \{1,\dots,n\}\;\exists x \in [x_{i-1},x_i]\;
\bigl(|x-t_i|\ge \delta(t_i)\bigr).
\]
nodes.failure_modes: A tagged partition fails to be $\delta$-fine precisely when some subinterval contains a point whose distance from its tag reaches or exceeds the available radius $\delta(t_i)$, so the corresponding subinterval is not contained in the prescribed open neighborhood around its tag.
nodes.failure_mode_decomposition:
\[
\underbrace{\exists i \in \{1,\dots,n\}\;\exists x \in [x_{i-1},x_i]}_{\text{choose offending subinterval and point}}
\;\underbrace{(|x-t_i|\ge \delta(t_i))}_{\text{distance not controlled by }\delta}.
\]
nodes.source_text: A $\delta$-fine tagged partition refines the interval so that each subinterval sits entirely inside the open ball centered at its tag with radius dictated by the gauge. This ensures that when summing local contributions, every evaluation point remains within a tolerance chosen for that location, a key requirement for gauge-based integral constructions. Failure occurs when a subinterval is too wide for the tolerance prescribed at its tag, so some point of the subinterval lies on or outside the allowed neighborhood.

nodes.name: Every Point Is Covered by a Tag
nodes.question: State the lemma: Every Point Is Covered by a Tag.
nodes.statement_display: Let $\dot{\mathcal{P}}=\{([x_{i-1},x_i],t_i)\}_{i=1}^n$ be a tagged partition of $[a,b]$ that is $\delta$-fine on $[a,b]$, and let $x\in[a,b]$. Then there exists $i\in\{1,\ldots,n\}$ such that $|x-t_i|<\delta(t_i)$.
nodes.statement_tex: Let $\dot{\mathcal{P}}=\{([x_{i-1},x_i],t_i)\}_{i=1}^n$ be a tagged partition of $[a,b]$ that is $\delta$-fine on $[a,b]$, and let $x\in[a,b]$. Then there exists $i\in\{1,\ldots,n\}$ such that $|x-t_i|<\delta(t_i)$.
nodes.source_text: A $\delta$-fine tagged partition matches each subinterval to a tag that lies inside the interval and records the gauge radius allowed at that tag. The lemma asserts that every point of the underlying interval falls within the gauge ball centered at one of the tags, so the gauge control at that tag can be applied to the point. This coverage property is the mechanism that transfers local control encoded by the gauge to arbitrary points of the interval.

nodes.name: Cousin's Theorem
nodes.question: State the theorem: Cousin's Theorem.
nodes.statement_display:
Every gauge $\delta:[a,b]\to(0,\infty)$ on a closed interval $[a,b]$
admits a $\delta$-fine tagged partition of $[a,b]$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Every gauge $\delta:[a,b]\to(0,\infty)$ on a closed interval $[a,b]$
admits a $\delta$-fine tagged partition of $[a,b]$.
nodes.formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } a,b \in \mathbb{R} \text{ with } a < b \text{ and let } \delta:[a,b]\to(0,\infty).\\
&\exists m \in \mathbb{N}\;\exists x_0,\ldots,x_m\;\exists t_1,\ldots,t_m\\
&\qquad\bigl(a=x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_m=b
\land \forall j\in\{1,\ldots,m\}\;(t_j\in[x_{j-1},x_j])\\
&\qquad\land
\forall j\in\{1,\ldots,m\}\;[x_{j-1},x_j]\subseteq
(t_j-\delta(t_j),t_j+\delta(t_j))\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } a,b \in \mathbb{R} \text{ with } a < b \text{ and let } \delta:[a,b]\to(0,\infty).\\
&\exists m \in \mathbb{N}\;\exists x_0,\ldots,x_m\;\exists t_1,\ldots,t_m\\
&\qquad\bigl(a=x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_m=b
\land \forall j\in\{1,\ldots,m\}\;(t_j\in[x_{j-1},x_j])\\
&\qquad\land
\forall j\in\{1,\ldots,m\}\;[x_{j-1},x_j]\subseteq
(t_j-\delta(t_j),t_j+\delta(t_j))\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } a,b \in \mathbb{R} \text{ with } a < b \text{ and let } \delta:[a,b]\to(0,\infty).\\
&\neg\Bigl(\text{there exists a }\delta\text{-fine tagged partition of }[a,b]\Bigr)\\
&\Longleftrightarrow
\forall m \in \mathbb{N}\;\forall x_0,\ldots,x_m\;\forall t_1,\ldots,t_m\\
&\qquad\Bigl[
\bigl(a=x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_m=b
\land \forall j\;(t_j\in[x_{j-1},x_j])\bigr)\\
&\qquad\Rightarrow
\exists j\in\{1,\ldots,m\}\;
[x_{j-1},x_j]\nsubseteq(t_j-\delta(t_j),t_j+\delta(t_j))
\Bigr].
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } a,b \in \mathbb{R} \text{ with } a < b \text{ and let } \delta:[a,b]\to(0,\infty).\\
&\neg\Bigl(\text{there exists a }\delta\text{-fine tagged partition of }[a,b]\Bigr)\\
&\Longleftrightarrow
\forall m \in \mathbb{N}\;\forall x_0,\ldots,x_m\;\forall t_1,\ldots,t_m\\
&\qquad\Bigl[
\bigl(a=x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_m=b
\land \forall j\;(t_j\in[x_{j-1},x_j])\bigr)\\
&\qquad\Rightarrow
\exists j\in\{1,\ldots,m\}\;
[x_{j-1},x_j]\nsubseteq(t_j-\delta(t_j),t_j+\delta(t_j))
\Bigr].
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.failure_modes:
Failure would mean that a positive gauge $\delta$ defeats every tagged
partition: no matter how the interval is subdivided and tagged, at least
one subinterval is not contained in the gauge neighborhood around its tag.
nodes.source_text:
The theorem is the gauge analogue of compactness for a closed interval.
Although the allowed radius may vary from point to point, finitely many
tagged subintervals can still be chosen so that each lies inside the
neighborhood dictated by its own tag. The standard bisection proof uses
the Nested Interval Property to rule out a gauge that defeats all finite
tagged partitions.
nodes.text_preview: [Cousin's Theorem] Every gauge $ :[a,b] (0, )$ on a closed interval $[a,b]$ admits a $ $-fine tagged partition of $[a,b]$.

nodes.name: Mesh of a Partition
nodes.question: State the definition: Mesh of a Partition.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$, and let $P=\{x_0,\ldots,x_n\}$ be a partition of $[a,b]$. A real number $m$ is the \emph{mesh} (or \emph{norm}) of $P$ if and only if
\[
m=\max_{1\le i\le n}(x_i - x_{i-1}).
\]
The mesh of $P$ is denoted by $\|P\|$ and is the unique $m$ satisfying this equivalence.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$, and let $P=\{x_0,\ldots,x_n\}$ be a partition of $[a,b]$. A real number $m$ is the \emph{mesh} (or \emph{norm}) of $P$ if and only if
\[
m=\max_{1\le i\le n}(x_i - x_{i-1}).
\]
The mesh of $P$ is denoted by $\|P\|$ and is the unique $m$ satisfying this equivalence.
nodes.formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } a,b\in\mathbb{R} \text{ with } a<b, \text{ and let } P=\{x_0,\ldots,x_n\} \text{ be a partition of } [a,b].\\
&m \text{ is the mesh of } P
\Longleftrightarrow
m=\max_{1\le i\le n}(x_i - x_{i-1}).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } a,b\in\mathbb{R} \text{ with } a<b, \text{ and let } P=\{x_0,\ldots,x_n\} \text{ be a partition of } [a,b].\\
&m \text{ is the mesh of } P
\Longleftrightarrow
m=\max_{1\le i\le n}(x_i - x_{i-1}).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } a,b\in\mathbb{R} \text{ with } a<b, \text{ and let } P=\{x_0,\ldots,x_n\} \text{ be a partition of } [a,b].\\
&\neg\bigl(m \text{ is the mesh of } P\bigr)
\Longleftrightarrow
\bigl(m<\max_{1\le i\le n}(x_i - x_{i-1})\bigr)\lor\bigl(m>\max_{1\le i\le n}(x_i - x_{i-1})\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } a,b\in\mathbb{R} \text{ with } a<b, \text{ and let } P=\{x_0,\ldots,x_n\} \text{ be a partition of } [a,b].\\
&\neg\bigl(m \text{ is the mesh of } P\bigr)
\Longleftrightarrow
\bigl(m<\max_{1\le i\le n}(x_i - x_{i-1})\bigr)\lor\bigl(m>\max_{1\le i\le n}(x_i - x_{i-1})\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_predicate:
\[
\neg\operatorname{NormOfPartition}(P,m)
\Longleftrightarrow
\bigl(m<\max_{1\le i\le n}(x_i - x_{i-1})\bigr)\lor\bigl(m>\max_{1\le i\le n}(x_i - x_{i-1})\bigr).
\]
nodes.failure_modes: Failure occurs either when $m$ underestimates the largest subinterval of $P$ or when $m$ overshoots that largest subinterval.
nodes.failure_mode_decomposition:
\[
\neg\operatorname{NormOfPartition}(P,m)
=
\underbrace{\bigl(m<\max_{1\le i\le n}(x_i - x_{i-1})\bigr)}_{\text{Underestimate}}
\;\lor\;
\underbrace{\bigl(m>\max_{1\le i\le n}(x_i - x_{i-1})\bigr)}_{\text{Overestimate}}.
\]
nodes.source_text: The mesh of a finite partition records the length of the widest subinterval and thus measures the resolution of the partition. Refining a partition strictly decreases or preserves this value, so a small mesh signals uniformly short subintervals, a critical input for Riemann and gauge integral estimates. Failure arises when a proposed number does not coincide with the actual maximum subinterval length, either because some subinterval is longer than the proposal or because all subintervals are shorter.

nodes.name: Refinement
nodes.question: State the definition: Refinement.
nodes.statement_display: Let [a,b] \subseteq \mathbb{R} and let P and Q be partitions of [a,b]. Then Q is a refinement of P if and only if every point of P is also a point of Q; equivalently, P \subseteq Q.
nodes.statement_tex: Let [a,b] \subseteq \mathbb{R} and let P and Q be partitions of [a,b]. Then Q is a refinement of P if and only if every point of P is also a point of Q; equivalently, P \subseteq Q.
nodes.formal:
\[
\forall x\;(x\in P \Rightarrow x\in Q).
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\forall x\;(x\in P \Rightarrow x\in Q).
\]
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\exists x\;(x\in P \land x\notin Q).
\]
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
\exists x\;(x\in P \land x\notin Q).
\]
nodes.failure_modes: Failure occurs precisely when some point listed in P fails to appear in Q, meaning Q omits at least one of the partition points it was supposed to inherit from P.
nodes.failure_mode_decomposition:
\[
\underbrace{\exists x \in P\; (x \notin Q)}_{\text{point of }P\text{ omitted by }Q}.
\]
nodes.source_text: Refinement formalizes the idea of subdividing an interval more finely without losing any previously chosen partition points. The condition P \subseteq Q guarantees that every subinterval defined by P is still represented when one passes to Q, allowing comparisons of sums or integrals computed on different meshes. Failure means that Q discards at least one point of P, so information tied to that breakpoint would be lost when transitioning between partitions.
nodes.text_preview: [Refinement] Let [a,b] and let P and Q be partitions of [a,b]. Then Q is a refinement of P if and only if every point of P is also a point of Q; equivalently, P Q.

nodes.name: Common Refinement
nodes.question: State the definition: Common Refinement.
nodes.statement_display: Let a<b and let P and Q be partitions of [a,b]. Their common refinement is the partition R obtained by taking the union of their node sets, so R = P \cup Q as sets of points in [a,b].
nodes.statement_tex: Let a<b and let P and Q be partitions of [a,b]. Their common refinement is the partition R obtained by taking the union of their node sets, so R = P \cup Q as sets of points in [a,b].
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&a<b\land P,Q\subseteq [a,b]\land R\subseteq [a,b]\\
&\Longrightarrow
\neg\bigl(\forall x\in\mathbb{R}\;(x\in R\Longleftrightarrow x\in P\cup Q)\bigr)\\
&\Longleftrightarrow
\bigl(\exists x\in R\;(x\notin P\cup Q)\bigr)
\lor
\bigl(\exists x\in P\cup Q\;(x\notin R)\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&a<b\land P,Q\subseteq [a,b]\land R\subseteq [a,b]\\
&\Longrightarrow
\neg\bigl(\forall x\in\mathbb{R}\;(x\in R\Longleftrightarrow x\in P\cup Q)\bigr)\\
&\Longleftrightarrow
\bigl(\exists x\in R\;(x\notin P\cup Q)\bigr)
\lor
\bigl(\exists x\in P\cup Q\;(x\notin R)\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.failure_modes: The claim that a partition R is the common refinement of P and Q fails exactly when R either contains a node not present in P \cup Q or omits a node that P \cup Q contributes. These are the only two structural ways in which R can differ from P \cup Q.
nodes.failure_mode_decomposition:
\[
R \ne P \cup Q
\Longleftrightarrow
\underbrace{\exists x \in R \setminus (P \cup Q)}_{\text{extra node}}
\;\lor\;
\underbrace{\exists x \in (P \cup Q) \setminus R}_{\text{missing node}}.
\]
nodes.source_text: Forming the common refinement of two partitions simply collects every partition point that either partition already uses, producing the unique partition whose nodes are exactly those existing ones. No new nodes are created beyond those already in P or Q, and none are discarded; the construction merely merges the lists so that later comparisons, such as evaluating tagged sums on both partitions simultaneously, can be performed without further adjustment.
nodes.text_preview: [Common Refinement] Let a<b and let P and Q be partitions of [a,b]. Their common refinement is the partition R obtained by taking the union of their node sets, so R = P Q as sets of points in [a,b].

nodes.name: Bounded on a Set
nodes.question: State the definition: Bounded on a Set.
nodes.statement_display: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and $f \colon A \to \mathbb{R}$. The function $f$ is bounded on $A$ if and only if there exists a constant $M>0$ such that $|f(x)| \le M$ for every $x \in A$.
nodes.statement_tex: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and $f \colon A \to \mathbb{R}$. The function $f$ is bounded on $A$ if and only if there exists a constant $M>0$ such that $|f(x)| \le M$ for every $x \in A$.
nodes.proposition:
\[
\operatorname{ContinuousOn}(f,[a,b])
\land \forall x\in[a,b]\;(f(x)\neq c)
\Longrightarrow
\Bigl[\bigl(\forall x\in[a,b]\; f(x)>c\bigr)
\lor
\bigl(\forall x\in[a,b]\; f(x)<c\bigr)\Bigr].
\]
nodes.formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } A \subseteq \mathbb{R} \text{ and } f \colon A \to \mathbb{R}.\\
&f \text{ is bounded on } A
\Longleftrightarrow
\exists M>0 \; \forall x \in A \; (|f(x)| \le M).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } A \subseteq \mathbb{R} \text{ and } f \colon A \to \mathbb{R}.\\
&f \text{ is bounded on } A
\Longleftrightarrow
\exists M>0 \; \forall x \in A \; (|f(x)| \le M).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } A \subseteq \mathbb{R} \text{ and } f \colon A \to \mathbb{R}.\\
&\neg(f \text{ bounded on } A)
\Longleftrightarrow
\forall M>0 \; \exists x \in A \; (|f(x)| > M).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } A \subseteq \mathbb{R} \text{ and } f \colon A \to \mathbb{R}.\\
&\neg(f \text{ bounded on } A)
\Longleftrightarrow
\forall M>0 \; \exists x \in A \; (|f(x)| > M).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.failure_modes: Failure occurs precisely when every proposed positive bound $M$ is exceeded by some value of $|f(x)|$ with $x \in A$.
nodes.failure_mode_decomposition:
\[
\underbrace{\forall M>0}_{\text{no admissible bound}}
\; \underbrace{\exists x \in A \; (|f(x)| > M)}_{\text{value exceeding } M}.
\]
nodes.source_text: The definition packages the informal idea that the values of $f$ stay within a fixed vertical strip over $A$. The witness $M$ serves as a uniform ceiling for the magnitudes $|f(x)|$, so once such an $M$ is known the graph of $f$ never escapes the band $[-M,M]$. Failure means that the outputs of $f$ grow without limit along some sequence of points inside $A$, so no finite strip can contain the entire image. Boundedness on a set is the foundational regularity hypothesis that underlies the boundedness and extreme value theorems for continuous functions on compact domains.
nodes.text_preview: [Bounded on a Set] Let $A $ and $f A $. The function $f$ is bounded on $A$ if and only if there exists a constant $M>0$ such that $|f(x)| M$ for every $x A$.

nodes.name: Boundedness Theorem
nodes.question: State the theorem: Boundedness Theorem.
nodes.statement_display: Let $a<b$ and let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous on $[a,b]$. Then there exists $M>0$ such that $|f(x)|\le M$ for every $x\in[a,b]$.
nodes.statement_tex: Let $a<b$ and let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous on $[a,b]$. Then there exists $M>0$ such that $|f(x)|\le M$ for every $x\in[a,b]$.
nodes.formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } a<b \text{ and } f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}.\\
&\Bigl(\forall c\in[a,b]\;\forall \varepsilon>0\;\exists \delta>0\;\forall x\in[a,b]\bigl(|x-c|<\delta \Rightarrow |f(x)-f(c)|<\varepsilon\bigr)\Bigr)\\
&\qquad\Longrightarrow \exists M>0\;\forall x\in[a,b]\;(|f(x)|\le M).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } a<b \text{ and } f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}.\\
&\Bigl(\forall c\in[a,b]\;\forall \varepsilon>0\;\exists \delta>0\;\forall x\in[a,b]\bigl(|x-c|<\delta \Rightarrow |f(x)-f(c)|<\varepsilon\bigr)\Bigr)\\
&\qquad\Longrightarrow \exists M>0\;\forall x\in[a,b]\;(|f(x)|\le M).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } a<b \text{ and } f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}.\\
&\Bigl(\forall c\in[a,b]\;\forall \varepsilon>0\;\exists \delta>0\;\forall x\in[a,b]\bigl(|x-c|<\delta \Rightarrow |f(x)-f(c)|<\varepsilon\bigr)\Bigr)\\
&\qquad\land \Bigl(\forall M>0\;\exists x\in[a,b]\;(|f(x)|>M)\Bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } a<b \text{ and } f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}.\\
&\Bigl(\forall c\in[a,b]\;\forall \varepsilon>0\;\exists \delta>0\;\forall x\in[a,b]\bigl(|x-c|<\delta \Rightarrow |f(x)-f(c)|<\varepsilon\bigr)\Bigr)\\
&\qquad\land \Bigl(\forall M>0\;\exists x\in[a,b]\;(|f(x)|>M)\Bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.failure_modes: Each component hypothesis is indispensable: without closedness (for instance $f(x)=1/x$ on $(0,1]$) continuity does not prevent divergence near the missing endpoint; without bounded domain (for example $f(x)=x$ on $[0,\infty)$) linear growth forces unbounded values; without continuity (take $f(0)=0$ and $f(x)=1/x$ for $x\in(0,1]$) the discontinuity at $0$ again creates arbitrarily large magnitudes.
nodes.proof_sketch:
Step 1. Assume for contradiction that $f$ is unbounded on $I = [a,b]$. This means that for each positive integer $n$, the set $\{x \in I : |f(x)| > n\}$ is nonempty. Therefore, for each $n \in \mathbb{N}$, there exists $x_n \in I$ such that $|f(x_n)| > n$. This constructs a sequence $(x_n)$ in $I$ with the property that the function values $f(x_n)$ grow without bound.
Step 2. Apply the Bolzano--Weierstrass Theorem to extract a convergent subsequence. Since $(x_n) \subseteq [a,b]$ and $[a,b]$ is bounded, the sequence $(x_n)$ is bounded. By the Bolzano--Weierstrass Theorem, every bounded sequence in $\mathbb{R}$ has a convergent subsequence. Therefore, there exists a subsequence $x_{n_k} \to x^*$ for some $x^* \in \mathbb{R}$.
Step 3. Show that the limit point belongs to the interval. Since $I = [a,b]$ is closed and each term $x_{n_k}$ belongs to $I$, the limit $x^*$ must also belong to $I$. This uses the fundamental property that closed sets contain the limits of all their convergent sequences.
Step 4. Apply continuity to obtain convergence in the image. Since $f$ is continuous at $x^* \in I$ and $x_{n_k} \to x^*$, the definition of continuity implies that $f(x_{n_k}) \to f(x^*)$. Therefore, the sequence $(f(x_{n_k}))$ converges to the finite value $f(x^*)$.
Step 5. Derive the contradiction. Every convergent sequence is bounded, so $(f(x_{n_k}))$ is bounded. However, from our construction in Step 1, we have $|f(x_{n_k})| > n_k$ for all $k$. Since $n_k$ is a subsequence of natural numbers, $n_k \geq k$, which means $|f(x_{n_k})| > k$ for all $k \in \mathbb{N}$. This shows that $(f(x_{n_k}))$ is unbounded, contradicting the fact that it is bounded. Therefore, our assumption that $f$ is unbounded must be false, and $f$ is bounded on $I$.
proof_steps: Step 1. Assume for contradiction that $f$ is unbounded on $I = [a,b]$. This means that for each positive integer $n$, the set $\{x \in I : |f(x)| > n\}$ is nonempty. Therefore, for each $n \in \mathbb{N}$, there exists $x_n \in I$ such that $|f(x_n)| > n$. This constructs a sequence $(x_n)$ in $I$ with the property that the function values $f(x_n)$ grow without bound.

Step 2. Apply the Bolzano--Weierstrass Theorem to extract a convergent subsequence. Since $(x_n) \subseteq [a,b]$ and $[a,b]$ is bounded, the sequence $(x_n)$ is bounded. By the Bolzano--Weierstrass Theorem, every bounded sequence in $\mathbb{R}$ has a convergent subsequence. Therefore, there exists a subsequence $x_{n_k} \to x^*$ for some $x^* \in \mathbb{R}$.

Step 3. Show that the limit point belongs to the interval. Since $I = [a,b]$ is closed and each term $x_{n_k}$ belongs to $I$, the limit $x^*$ must also belong to $I$. This uses the fundamental property that closed sets contain the limits of all their convergent sequences.

Step 4. Apply continuity to obtain convergence in the image. Since $f$ is continuous at $x^* \in I$ and $x_{n_k} \to x^*$, the definition of continuity implies that $f(x_{n_k}) \to f(x^*)$. Therefore, the sequence $(f(x_{n_k}))$ converges to the finite value $f(x^*)$.

Step 5. Derive the contradiction. Every convergent sequence is bounded, so $(f(x_{n_k}))$ is bounded. However, from our construction in Step 1, we have $|f(x_{n_k})| > n_k$ for all $k$. Since $n_k$ is a subsequence of natural numbers, $n_k \geq k$, which means $|f(x_{n_k})| > k$ for all $k \in \mathbb{N}$. This shows that $(f(x_{n_k}))$ is unbounded, contradicting the fact that it is bounded. Therefore, our assumption that $f$ is unbounded must be false, and $f$ is bounded on $I$.
nodes.proof_idea: The proof proceeds by contradiction, assuming $f$ is unbounded and deriving a contradiction through a three-stage argument: image to domain to image. First, unboundedness in the image allows construction of a sequence $(x_n)$ with $|f(x_n)| > n$. Second, the Bolzano--Weierstrass Theorem operates on this sequence in the domain $[a,b]$ to extract a convergent subsequence whose limit remains in the closed interval. Third, continuity pulls the convergent subsequence back to the image, where convergence implies boundedness, contradicting the constructed unboundedness. The compactness of $[a,b]$ (closed and bounded) is essential: boundedness enables Bolzano--Weierstrass, and closedness ensures the limit stays in the domain.
nodes.source_text: Continuity ties nearby inputs to nearby outputs, and the compact interval $[a,b]$ can be covered by finitely many neighborhoods in which the oscillation of $f$ is controlled; taking the maximal oscillation among this finite cover supplies a global bound. Failure typically arises when the domain lacks compactness: an omitted endpoint allows the function to escape control near the hole, an unbounded interval lets values diverge along the ray, and a point of discontinuity can spike the function without upsetting nearby behavior elsewhere.
nodes.text_preview: [Boundedness Theorem] Let $a<b$ and let $f:[a,b] $ be continuous on $[a,b]$. Then there exists $M>0$ such that $|f(x)| M$ for every $x $.

nodes.name: Absolute Maximum and Absolute Minimum
nodes.question: State the definition: Absolute Maximum and Absolute Minimum.
nodes.statement_display: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $f \colon A \to \mathbb{R}$. A point $x^{*} \in A$ is an absolute maximum of $f$ on $A$ if and only if $f(x^{*}) \ge f(x)$ for every $x \in A$. A point $x_{*} \in A$ is an absolute minimum of $f$ on $A$ if and only if $f(x_{*}) \le f(x)$ for every $x \in A$.
nodes.statement_tex: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $f \colon A \to \mathbb{R}$. A point $x^{*} \in A$ is an absolute maximum of $f$ on $A$ if and only if $f(x^{*}) \ge f(x)$ for every $x \in A$. A point $x_{*} \in A$ is an absolute minimum of $f$ on $A$ if and only if $f(x_{*}) \le f(x)$ for every $x \in A$.
nodes.proposition:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&(x^{*} \in A) \land \operatorname{Maximum}(f(x^{*}), f(A))
\Longleftrightarrow (x^{*} \in A) \land \forall x \in A\; (f(x) \le f(x^{*})),\\
&(x_{*} \in A) \land \operatorname{Minimum}(f(x_{*}), f(A))
\Longleftrightarrow (x_{*} \in A) \land \forall x \in A\; (f(x_{*}) \le f(x)).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.formal:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } A \subseteq \mathbb{R},\; f \colon A \to \mathbb{R},\; x^{*}, x_{*} \in A.\\
&x^{*} \text{ is an absolute maximum of } f \text{ on } A
\Longleftrightarrow \forall x \in A\; (f(x) \le f(x^{*})).\\
&x_{*} \text{ is an absolute minimum of } f \text{ on } A
\Longleftrightarrow \forall x \in A\; (f(x_{*}) \le f(x)).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } A \subseteq \mathbb{R},\; f \colon A \to \mathbb{R},\; x^{*}, x_{*} \in A.\\
&x^{*} \text{ is an absolute maximum of } f \text{ on } A
\Longleftrightarrow \forall x \in A\; (f(x) \le f(x^{*})).\\
&x_{*} \text{ is an absolute minimum of } f \text{ on } A
\Longleftrightarrow \forall x \in A\; (f(x_{*}) \le f(x)).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_display:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } A \subseteq \mathbb{R},\; f \colon A \to \mathbb{R},\; x^{*}, x_{*} \in \mathbb{R}.\\
&x^{*} \text{ fails to be an absolute maximum of } f \text{ on } A\\
&\Longleftrightarrow (x^{*} \notin A) \lor \bigl(\exists x \in A\; (f(x) > f(x^{*}))\bigr).\\
&x_{*} \text{ fails to be an absolute minimum of } f \text{ on } A\\
&\Longleftrightarrow (x_{*} \notin A) \lor \bigl(\exists x \in A\; (f(x_{*}) > f(x))\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]

\[

&x^{*} \text{ fails to be an absolute maximum of } f \text{ on } A\\
&\Longleftrightarrow (x^{*} \notin A) \lor \bigl(\exists x \in A\; (f(x) > f(x^{*}))\bigr).\\
&x_{*} \text{ fails to be an absolute minimum of } f \text{ on } A\\
&\Longleftrightarrow (x_{*} \notin A) \lor \bigl(\exists x \in A\; (f(x_{*}) > f(x))\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.negation_predicate:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\neg\bigl[(x^{*} \in A) \land \operatorname{Maximum}(f(x^{*}), f(A))\bigr]
\Longleftrightarrow (x^{*} \notin A) \lor \bigl(\exists x \in A\; (f(x) > f(x^{*}))\bigr),\\
&\neg\bigl[(x_{*} \in A) \land \operatorname{Minimum}(f(x_{*}), f(A))\bigr]
\Longleftrightarrow (x_{*} \notin A) \lor \bigl(\exists x \in A\; (f(x_{*}) > f(x))\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
nodes.failure_modes: An alleged absolute maximum can fail because the proposed point lies outside the domain $A$, or because some $x \in A$ produces a strictly larger function value. An alleged absolute minimum fails for the analogous reasons, either by leaving the domain or by admitting a point in $A$ with a strictly smaller value.
nodes.source_text: An absolute maximum identifies the highest value that $f$ attains on its domain $A$, together with a point of attainment, and the absolute minimum identifies the lowest attained value. These extrema describe the global ceiling and floor of the graph restricted to $A$, so they are the endpoints of the vertical range. Failure occurs either when the candidate point is not part of the domain or when another domain point produces a superior value, reflecting that global optimization is sensitive to both membership and comparative size. Geometrically the absolute maximum sits at the tallest point of the graph above $A$, while the absolute minimum sits at the lowest point.

nodes.name: Extreme Value Theorem
nodes.question: State the theorem: Extreme Value Theorem.
nodes.statement_display: Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$, and let $f:[a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuous. There exist points $x_m,x_M \in [a,b]$ such that $f(x_m) \le f(x) \le f(x_M)$ for every $x \in [a,b]$. Equivalently, $f$ attains both an absolute maximum and an absolute minimum on $[a,b]$.
nodes.statement_tex: Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$, and let $f:[a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuous. There exist points $x_m,x_M \in [a,b]$ such that $f(x_m) \le f(x) \le f(x_M)$ for every $x \in [a,b]$. Equivalently, $f$ attains both an absolute maximum and an absolute minimum on $[a,b]$.
nodes.failure_modes:
The theorem can fail if the compact interval hypothesis is lost, or if
the function is not continuous on the whole interval. Without compactness
an extremal value may exist only as a limiting value, and without
continuity the graph may jump over its supremum or infimum.
nodes.source_text: Continuity on a closed and bounded interval forces the image of the interval to inherit the compact features of the domain. The function cannot escape to infinity or oscillate without settling, so it must reach both its highest and lowest values somewhere in the interval. When the hypotheses fail, the typical obstruction is the loss of compactness: on an open interval the function might drift off without achieving its supremum, and without continuity it may jump over candidate extrema.
